title: Convertitore di Peso
description: Converti peso tra kg, libbre, once, grammi, tonnellate e altro. Conversioni di peso precise per cucina, sport e uso quotidiano.

faq.title: Domande Frequenti
faq.q1: Quante libbre ci sono in un chilogrammo?
faq.a1: 1 kg = 2,20462 libbre (lb). 1 libbra = 0,453592 kg. Per una conversione rapida: moltiplica i kg per 2,2 per ottenere le libbre. Esempio: 70 kg × 2,2 = 154 lb. Per convertire libbre in kg: dividi per 2,2 (o moltiplica per 0,454).
faq.q2: Come si convertono le once in grammi?
faq.a2: 1 oncia (oz) = 28,3495 grammi. 1 grammo = 0,03527 once. 1 kg = 35,274 once. Le once sono usate per misurare alimenti nelle ricette americane e i metalli preziosi (oro, argento). Nota: l'oncia troy (per metalli preziosi) = 31,1035 g, diversa dall'oncia avoirdupois standard (28,35 g).
faq.q3: Cosa è una libbra vs una pietra (stone)?
faq.a3: 1 stone = 14 libbre = 6,35 kg. La stone è usata nel Regno Unito per esprimere il peso corporeo. Una persona di 70 kg = 154 lb = 11 stone. 1 libbra = 16 once = 453,6 g. In Italia e nella maggior parte d'Europa non si usa né la libbra né la stone — si usano kg e g.
faq.q4: Come si converte il peso corporeo tra kg e lb?
faq.a4: Kg a lb: moltiplica per 2,205. Lb a kg: moltiplica per 0,454. Pesi di riferimento: 50 kg = 110 lb, 60 kg = 132 lb, 70 kg = 154 lb, 80 kg = 176 lb, 90 kg = 198 lb, 100 kg = 220 lb. Per confrontare con standard americani di salute, converti sempre in kg per usare le soglie IMC OMS.
faq.q5: Qual è la differenza tra peso e massa?
faq.a5: In fisica, la massa è la quantità di materia (misurata in kg) — costante ovunque. Il peso è la forza gravitazionale sulla massa (misurata in Newton) — dipende dalla gravità. Sulla Luna la massa è la stessa ma il peso è 1/6. Nella vita quotidiana e in cucina, 'peso' e 'massa' sono usati indifferentemente e misurati in kg, g o lb.
faq.q6: Come si convertono le unità di peso in cucina?
faq.a6: 1 kg = 1.000 g. 1 libbra americana (lb) = 454 g ≈ 450 g per la cucina. 1 oncia americana (oz) = 28 g ≈ 30 g per la cucina. Conversioni utili: 2 lb di carne = circa 900 g. 1 oz di cioccolato = circa 28 g. 4 oz di formaggio = circa 113 g. 1/2 lb = circa 225 g.
faq.q7: Come funzionano le unità di peso per i metalli preziosi?
faq.a7: I metalli preziosi usano l'oncia troy: 1 oncia troy = 31,1035 g (vs 28,35 g dell'oncia normale). Il prezzo dell'oro è quotato in USD/oncia troy. 1 kg d'oro = 32,15 once troy. I carati (usati per l'oro) misurano la purezza, non il peso: oro 18 carati = 18/24 = 75% oro puro. Non confondere con i carati dei diamanti (che misurano il peso: 1 carato = 0,2 g).
faq.q8: Come si misurano i carichi nei trasporti?
faq.a8: Nei trasporti si usano: tonnellate metriche (1 t = 1.000 kg) per carichi pesanti. Chilogrammi per carichi medi. Per aereo: kg (limite bagaglio tipico 20-23 kg). Spedizioni internazionali: il peso può essere 'volumetrico' — max(peso reale, peso volumetrico), dove peso volumetrico = lunghezza×larghezza×altezza(cm) / 5.000 per spedizioni aeree.

content.whatIs:
A weight converter translates between the full spectrum of mass and weight measurement units — from micrograms and milligrams used in pharmacy and nutrition, through grams and kilograms for everyday use, to metric tons and short tons for industrial applications, including the full range of imperial units (ounces, pounds, stones) and specialized units like the troy ounce used in precious metals trading. Enter any weight in any unit and instantly see the equivalent in any other unit with precision across all scales.

Weight measurement systems evolved independently across civilizations and were standardized only recently — and incompletely. The metric system's kilogram, based on the physical properties of water and now defined through fundamental physical constants, coexists with the 2,000-year-old Roman pound, the medieval British stone, the ancient troy ounce of alchemists and goldsmiths, and the avoirdupois ounce of everyday commerce. Each system persists because specific industries and cultures built their practices around it. Pharmaceutical dosing uses milligrams; grocery shopping in the U.S. uses ounces and pounds; body weight in the UK is discussed in stones; commodity trading uses metric tons; gold and silver are priced in troy ounces.

The need to convert between these systems is not diminishing — if anything, global commerce and digital shopping have increased the frequency of weight conversions. A U.S. consumer buying imported food sees gram labels; an investor reading gold market news deals in troy ounces; a person monitoring their health may track body weight in kilograms (as medical professionals require) while thinking in pounds from daily habit. This converter handles all these conversions with exact or high-precision factors.
content.formula:
SI metric (exact):
1 metric ton (t) = 1,000 kilograms (kg)
1 kilogram = 1,000 grams (g)
1 gram = 1,000 milligrams (mg)
1 milligram = 1,000 micrograms (μg)

Imperial / U.S. customary (avoirdupois):
1 short ton (U.S.) = 2,000 pounds (lb)
1 stone = 14 pounds (UK body weight)
1 pound = 16 ounces (oz)
1 ounce = 28.3495 grams
1 pound = 453.59237 grams (exact by definition)

Cross-system conversions:
1 kilogram = 2.20462 pounds
1 pound = 0.453592 kilograms
1 ounce = 28.3495 grams
1 gram = 0.035274 ounces
1 metric ton = 2,204.62 pounds = 1.10231 short tons
1 short ton = 907.185 kg = 0.907185 metric tons
1 UK long ton = 2,240 pounds = 1,016.05 kg = 1.016 metric tons

Troy weight (precious metals):
1 troy pound = 12 troy ounces = 373.242 grams
1 troy ounce = 31.1035 grams
1 troy ounce = 1.09714 avoirdupois ounces
content.example:
Example 1 — Body weight conversion:
75 kilograms to pounds: 75 × 2.20462 = 165.35 pounds
In stones: 165.35 / 14 = 11.81 stone = 11 stone 11.4 pounds

Example 2 — Gold price calculation:
Gold at $1,950 per troy ounce.
1 troy ounce = 31.1035 grams.
$1,950 / 31.1035 = $62.70 per gram.
100 grams of gold = 100 × $62.70 = $6,270.
1 kilogram gold = $62,700.

Example 3 — Pharmaceutical dose:
Medication dosed at 10 mg/kg for a 68 kg patient.
Dose = 10 × 68 = 680 mg = 0.68 grams.
If tablet is 200 mg: 680/200 = 3.4 tablets (take 3.5 tablets or adjust to nearest half-tablet).

Example 4 — Shipping weight:
Package: 3.2 kg
In pounds: 3.2 × 2.20462 = 7.05 pounds
In ounces: 7.05 × 16 = 112.8 ounces
For U.S. postal shipping: 7.05 pounds (rounds to next pound = 8 lb for postal rate).
content.howItWorks_1: Enter the numerical value of the weight you want to convert and select the source unit from the 'From' dropdown. The converter supports milligrams, grams, kilograms, metric tons, ounces (avoirdupois), pounds, stones, short tons, long tons, and troy ounces. Decimal values are supported; negative values are not applicable for weight.
content.howItWorks_2: Select the target unit from the 'To' dropdown. All conversions use exact or high-precision factors derived from internationally defined conversion relationships. The pound-to-kilogram conversion is exact (0.45359237 kg/lb) because the pound is legally defined in metric terms since 1959.
content.howItWorks_3: The converted value appears instantly. For very large values (tons to micrograms) or very small values (micrograms to tons), results are displayed in scientific notation to maintain readability. Swap the From and To units to perform the reverse conversion.
content.whyItMatters:
Weight conversion errors in medicine and pharmacy have historically caused serious harm. The classic pediatric dosing error involves confusion between pounds and kilograms: a child's weight recorded in pounds (60 lbs) interpreted as kilograms gives a dose 2.2× too high. A 2009 Joint Commission Sentinel Event Alert cited weight-based dosing errors as a significant patient safety risk, specifically highlighting kg/lb confusion as a root cause in overdose incidents. Healthcare settings now require body weight to be recorded in kilograms exclusively, specifically to eliminate this conversion error — but transitions between systems still create risk at system boundaries.

In international trade and commodity markets, weight unit confusion has significant financial consequences. When oil contracts specified in metric tons are processed by software expecting short tons, the apparent volume is 10% different — a difference of hundreds of thousands of dollars per tanker load at scale. Agricultural commodity contracts (grain, soybeans, cotton) are denominated in metric tons on international markets and in short tons or bushels on U.S. domestic markets, requiring constant conversion in international trading. A systematic 0.1% conversion error in a commodity trading desk processing millions of tons per month accumulates rapidly into meaningful P&L impact.

For consumers, weight conversion affects purchasing decisions in grocery shopping, shipping cost estimation, and recipe scaling. A recipe specifying 200g of butter requires a gram-based kitchen scale or a precise conversion to tablespoons (200g ÷ 14.2g per tablespoon = approximately 14 tablespoons). International shipping rate structures are based on weight in kilograms at international couriers, but the USPS uses pounds. Comparing costs requires accurate conversion. Nutritional analysis of a recipe requires knowing the gram weight of each ingredient even when measured by volume — which requires knowing density, but weight conversion is the starting point.
content.limitations:
This converter covers common weight units including all metric SI prefixed units, avoirdupois imperial units (ounces, pounds, stones, short tons, long tons), metric tons, and troy ounces for precious metals. It does not cover some historical or highly specialized units that occasionally appear in technical literature: the grain (1/7000 pound = 64.799 mg, used in ballistics and pharmacy historically), the carat (200 mg exactly, used for gemstone weight — note this is different from gold 'karat' which measures gold purity), the hundredweight (cwt, which equals 100 lb in the U.S. but 112 lb in the UK), and the pennyweight (dwt = 1/20 troy ounce = 1.555 g, used in jewelry).

This converter handles mass/weight only — it does not perform density-based conversions between weight and volume. Converting '500 grams of flour to cups' requires knowing flour density, which varies with packing method. Converting 'liters of oil to kilograms' requires knowing the specific oil's density. These weight-to-volume conversions require density as an intermediate step and are not handled by a unit converter.

Note on 'weight' vs 'mass' in everyday use: this converter treats the terms interchangeably as is standard in everyday usage. In scientific and engineering contexts, mass (in kilograms) and weight (in newtons) are distinct quantities. The pound-force (lbf) is a unit of force/weight, while the pound-mass (lbm) is a unit of mass — the distinction matters in aerospace and mechanical engineering. This converter uses mass units throughout and does not convert between force and mass units.
content.tips_1: For quick kg-to-pound mental conversion, the 2.2 multiplier is accurate within 0.2%. 70 kg ≈ 154 lbs (exact: 154.32). For pounds-to-kg, divide by 2.2 — or use the slightly more memorable 'multiply by 0.45.' 150 lbs × 0.45 = 67.5 kg (exact: 68.04 kg). For body weight specifically, the difference between 'approximately 68 kg' and 'exactly 68.04 kg' is clinically irrelevant.
content.tips_2: In cooking and baking, use a kitchen scale in grams rather than volume measures for precision. Flour is notorious for inconsistency: one cup can be 120g or 165g depending on whether it was sifted, spooned, or packed. A recipe that specifies 250g of flour will produce consistent results regardless of who is measuring; a recipe that specifies 2 cups may fail for bakers with a heavier scooping technique. Professional and food science recipes are always weight-based for this reason.
content.tips_3: When calculating body mass index (BMI), you need weight in kilograms and height in meters: BMI = kg/m². If you know your weight in pounds and height in inches: BMI = (lbs / in²) × 703. First convert pounds to kilograms (divide by 2.2046) and height in inches to meters (multiply by 0.0254). Alternatively: lbs × 0.453592 = kg, and height in feet/inches to meters = (feet × 0.3048) + (inches × 0.0254).
content.tips_4: For shipping, always confirm whether the carrier uses volumetric weight (dimensional weight) in addition to actual weight. Major carriers (FedEx, UPS, DHL) charge the greater of actual weight or dimensional weight = (L × W × H cm) / 5,000 for most services. A lightweight but bulky package may be charged based on its volume rather than its mass. Convert the dimensional weight to the same unit as actual weight and compare — the higher value determines the shipping charge.
